title: React 18 + Vite Frontend Architecture — Quick Check

questions.q: What is the difference between `ReactDOM.render()` and `ReactDOM.createRoot()` in terms of rendering behaviour?
questions.a: ReactDOM.render() is the legacy synchronous API — the scheduler runs in blocking mode, holding the main thread for the entire render pass. ReactDOM.createRoot() opts into concurrent rendering, enabling the scheduler to yield, prioritize, and interrupt renders. All of React 18's concurrent features (`startTransition`, `useTransition`, `useDeferredValue`) are no-ops with the legacy API — they compile and run without errors but have no effect on scheduling.

questions.q: What does `startTransition` do, and which specific Meridian interaction is it designed to protect?
questions.a: startTransition marks a state update as non-urgent, allowing React to interrupt and re-plan it if a higher-priority update (like a user keystroke) arrives. In Meridian, it protects the editor experience: when an AI panel update (loading 50+ suggestions) is wrapped in startTransition, a user typing in the Lexical editor still gets immediate visual feedback — the AI panel update yields to the keystroke rather than blocking it.

questions.q: What is Lexical's 'EditorState as the source of truth' pattern, and why is mutating the DOM directly a bug even if it produces the correct visual result?
questions.a: Lexical maintains a reconciliation model: it diffs the previous EditorState against the new one and applies the minimal DOM changes needed. If you mutate the DOM directly, Lexical's next reconcile will overwrite your change because the EditorState still reflects the old content. The DOM is Lexical's output, not its input — the editor state always drives the DOM, never the reverse.

questions.q: How does Vite's development server differ from Webpack's dev server in terms of module serving, and what specific development benefit does this produce?
questions.a: Vite's dev server serves ES modules directly to the browser without bundling — the browser handles module graph resolution natively. Webpack bundles all modules into one or more chunks before serving. Consequence: Vite's cold start time scales with the number of changed files (milliseconds per module), not total project size. Hot module replacement replaces the changed module only. In large projects, this produces sub-100ms HMR vs. Webpack's multi-second full bundle rebuilds.
